texts.orig: Membrane  based  separation  technology  is  well  considered  as  a promising option for carbon dioxide (CO2) capture due to its advantages of  energy-efficient  and  environmental  friendliness  compared  to  the conventional  energy-intensive  methods  include  cryogenic  distillation and  amine-based  scrubbing  [1 -4].  Polymeric  membranes  have  been commercially  used  in  CO2  separation,  such  as  cellulose  acetate  and polyimides, while their widespread applications are somewhat limited due to two major challenges [5,6]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membrane with high CO2 separation performance were fabricated by pyrolysis of a novel hydroxyl-containing polyetherimide (BAHPPF-6FDA type HPEI) precursor. The thermal reactive orthoOH  group  in  HPEI  resulted  in  the  structural  conversion  when  thermal  treated  at  450 ◦ C,  which  simultaneously improved the chain rigidity and FFV of the precursor membrane. The structural evolution from HPEI precursor to intermediate polymer till CMS membrane was detected via TGA-MS, Py-GC/MS, FT-IR and WAXD. The pore structure, CO2 separation performance and aging behavior of CMS membranes were characterized and analyzed by physisorption and gas permeation measurements. Results indicated that the structural properties of the intermediate TR-polymers, which depend on the thermal treatment protocol, have an obvious influence on the CMS membrane performance. Increased thermal soak time of HPEI at 450 ◦ C leads to the increased chain rigidity and FFV, resulting in a more open pore structure and higher CO2  permeability of derived CMS membranes. In addition, the prolonged soak time at 450 ◦ C or increased pyrolysis temperature would result in the shrinkage of pore structure in CMS membranes and the enhanced molecular sieving performance. The high permeation CMS membrane prepared at the optimal thermal treatment protocol shows good performance stability under the mixed gas (50:50 mixture of CO2/N2 or CO2/CH4) feeds. The relative high CO2 permeability of about 10000 Barrer and CO2/N2 (or CH4) selectivity over 30 were obtained after 60 days aging under vacuum, which exhibits a good potential for CO2  separation application.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A plenty of polymeric materials have been used for the fabrication of CMS membranes, including phenolic resin [20,21], cellulose and derivatives [22,23], poly (acrylonitrile) [24], polyimide and derivatives [25 -30],  poly  (aryl  ether  ketone)  [18,31,32]  and  so  on.  It  has  been indicated that the CMS membranes derived from different polymeric precursors have great differences in microstructure and gas separation performance [10,27,28,30,32 -34]. Kiyono et al. [30] reported the gas separation performance of CMS membrane derived from two polymeric precursors  (6FDA/BPDA-DAM  and  commercialized  polymer  called Matrimid).  The  6FDA/BPDA-DAM-based  CMS  membrane  showed  a more open structure and CO2 permeability of 7170 Barrer that is almost seven times greater than a CMS membrane derived from Matrimid when prepared at same condition. Hu et al. [28] also found that the gas separation performance of ODPA-FDA-based CMS membrane was higher than that of Matrimid-based membrane. Given the facts above, the selection of suitable precursors has been considered as a crucial factor in the preparation of high-performance CMS membranes. And the structural properties of polymeric precursors have significant effects on the gas separation performance of CMS membranes [25 -29,32,35]. Ma et al. synthesized  two  microporous  polyimides  based  on  diamine  isomer monomers to prepare CMS membranes [29]. The molecular configuration  difference  between  monomers  affects  the  chain  arrangement  of polymer precursors, resulting in the great difference of gas permeability between the CMS membranes. The isomer-tailored CMS membranes also be  reported  by  Qiu  et  al.  [36].  The  multiscale  natures  of  polymeric precursors based the molecular structure and chain conformation were discussed in our previous work [37]. These works provide a molecular insight into the structural design of the polymeric precursors. Furthermore, enhancing the fractional free volume (FFV) of polymer precursors by introducing the bulky bridging groups (CH3  or CF3) into polymeric chains has been proved to be beneficial to improving the gas permeability of derived CMS membranes [25,30,35]. Koros et al. reported a series  of  high  permeation  CMS  membranes  with  open  microporous structures, which were derived from the high FFV polyimide precursors containing  6FDA  units  [27,30,38].  The  open  microporous  structures were formed at pyrolysis temperatures greater than 500 ◦ C due to the decomposition of the 6F group and its escape from the membrane as gaseous products [30,39]. The influence of chain rigidity of precursors on gas transport properties of CMS membrane has also been revealed in previous works [28,40,41]. Hazazi et al. and Hu et al. separately reported the CMS membranes derived from the polyimide precursors with the highly rigidity aromatic groups [28,41]. The rigid molecular structure (e.g. fluorene group) of highly aromatic polyimide precursors leads to a more open CMS structure due to its loose chain packing [28]. These works  indicated  that  the  gas  separation  performances  of  CMS  membranes could be improved by exquisite controlling the structural properties of polymeric precursors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  recent  years,  the  functionalized  polymer  containing  reactive functional groups (such as -COOH, -OH and norbornene) [26,27,33,42] have been designed and employed as precursor materials for preparation of  high-performance  CMS membranes due to their unique molecular structure. The functional groups of precursors usually occur a thermal-induced chemical reaction to form the intermediate polymer with more rigid structure before the decomposition and fragmentation of  polymer  backbone,  and  would  affect  the  pore  structure  and  gas permeation properties of final CMS membranes. Koros et al. reported that the high permeability of CMS membrane derived from carboxyl-containing copolyimide due to the formation of bulky and rigid crosslinked  chain  structure  in  polymeric  precursor  during  thermal treatment [27]. Kanezashi et al. pyrolyzed the norbornene-functionalized polyimide to prepare highly permeable CMS membranes [33]. The introduction of norbornene leads to the formation of cross-linked structure during thermal treatment, which contributes to the  high  permeation  properties  of  CMS  membranes.  Besides  crosslinking,  thermal-induced  chemical  rearrangement  method  has  been applied  to  improve  the  chain  rigidity  of  polymer  precursors.  Pinnau et  al.  firstly  reported  the  ortho-hydroxyl  functionalized  polyimide derived CMS membranes [26,43,44]. The benzoxazole segment, formed by thermal conversion between ortho-OH group and amide carbonyl group at 400 -450 ◦ C, would simultaneously improve the chain rigidity and FFV of the polymer precursor [45]. The more open structure and high gas permeability were obtained by the CMS membrane derived from the hydroxyl-functionalized polyimide compared to the hydroxyl-free polyimide [46]. These previous efforts reveal that precursor functionalization is an efficient method that enhances the chain rigidity and FFV of the precursor to improve CMS membrane performance. However, the effect of intermediate polymer structure on CMS membrane performance and  the  formation  mechanism  of  open  CMS structure  derived  from  functionalized  polymer  precursors  need  to  be further investigated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently,  we  reported  a  series  of  novel  hydroxyl-containing  poly (ether-imide)s (HPEIs) with good membrane-forming and mechanical properties [47]. Among them, the BAHPPF-6FDA type HPEI shows a loose chain structure due to its relatively high FFV. The thermal treatment  of  BAHPPF-6FDA  at  450 ◦ C  leads  to  the  formation  of  poly (ether-benzoxazole) (PEBO) due to thermal rearrangement (TR) reaction, and the TR membranes exhibited the more rigid chain structure and enhanced gas permeation properties. This FFV characteristic and the  rigid  structure  formed  by  thermal  rearrangement  suggest  that BAHPPF-6FDA can be used as a precursor for preparing the high-performance  CMS  membrane.  Herein,  we  prepared  CMS  membranes by the pyrolysis of BAHPPF-6FDA type HPEI precursor and tried to improve the gas separation performance of the CMS membranes by tuning the structural properties of the formed intermediate TR-polymers.  The  structural  evolution  from  HPEI  precursor  to  intermediate TR-polymer till CMS was detected via different characterization techniques.  The  structural  properties  of  intermediate  TR-polymers, controlled by varying thermal treatment protocols, were analyzed by structural characterization and molecular simulation. The microstructure, pore structure, CO2 separation performance and aging behavior of CMS membranes were tested and analyzed.

Among them, the BAHPPF-6FDA type HPEI shows a loose chain structure due to its relatively high FFV. The thermal treatment  of  BAHPPF-6FDA  at  450 ◦ C  leads  to  the  formation  of  poly (ether-benzoxazole) (PEBO) due to thermal rearrangement (TR) reaction, and the TR membranes exhibited the more rigid chain structure and enhanced gas permeation properties. This FFV characteristic and the  rigid  structure  formed  by  thermal  rearrangement  suggest  that BAHPPF-6FDA can be used as a precursor for preparing the high-performance  CMS  membrane.  Herein,  we  prepared  CMS  membranes by the pyrolysis of BAHPPF-6FDA type HPEI precursor and tried to improve the gas separation performance of the CMS membranes by tuning the structural properties of the formed intermediate TR-polymers.  The  structural  evolution  from  HPEI  precursor  to  intermediate TR-polymer till CMS was detected via different characterization techniques.  The  structural  properties  of  intermediate  TR-polymers, controlled by varying thermal treatment protocols, were analyzed by structural characterization and molecular simulation. The microstructure, pore structure, CO2 separation performance and aging behavior of CMS membranes were tested and analyzed.

The equimolar ratio of dianhydride (6FDA) was added and the resulted mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h to form hydroxyl-containing poly (ether-amic acid) (HPEAA) solution (15 wt%). Following the obtained HPEAA solutions were casted on glass plates and dried at 40 ◦ C for 12 h under a dry and dust-free environment, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ◦ C and 120 ◦ C for 24 h to remove the residual solvent. The obtained membranes (90 ± 5 μ m) were cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm 2 pieces. And then membranes were heated at a rate of 3 ◦ C/min from room temperature to 350 ◦ C and held for 60 min. This protocol ensures full imidization of the sample before rearrangement. The BAHPPF-6FDA membrane shows a glass transition temperature ( T g) of 339 ◦ C, as shown in Fig. S1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The HPEI membrane was heated in a tubular furnace from room temperature to 450 ◦ C at 3 ◦ C/min under a N2 flow of 300 mL/min for thermal  rearrangement.  The  currently  proposed  reaction  route  for thermal rearrangement was shown in Fig. 1. The samples were soaked at 450 ◦ C for a given time (0 h, 1 h and 2 h, respectively), then cooling to room temperature. The TR membranes will be notated as ' TR-T(x) ' , where T represents thermal treatment temperature, and the x represents the soaking time. For instance, the TR membrane obtained at 450 ◦ C for 1 h were labeled as ' TR-450 (1h) ' . In addition, the weight loss of each membrane during the thermal treatment was monitored and used to estimate the thermal conversion degree of HPEI-to-PEBO.
texts.text: The HPEI membrane was heated in a tubular furnace from room temperature to 450 ◦ C at 3 ◦ C/min under a N2 flow of 300 mL/min for thermal  rearrangement.  The  currently  proposed  reaction  route  for thermal rearrangement was shown in Fig. 1. The samples were soaked at 450 ◦ C for a given time (0 h, 1 h and 2 h, respectively), then cooling to room temperature. The TR membranes will be notated as ' TR-T(x) ' , where T represents thermal treatment temperature, and the x represents the soaking time. For instance, the TR membrane obtained at 450 ◦ C for 1 h were labeled as ' TR-450 (1h) ' . In addition, the weight loss of each membrane during the thermal treatment was monitored and used to estimate the thermal conversion degree of HPEI-to-PEBO.

Approximately  0.5 mg  of  sample  was  loaded  in  the  micro-pyrolyzer  (CDS6200,  CDS Analytical  Inc.,  USA),  and  was  heated  to  the  required  temperature (550 ◦ C and 700 ◦ C) at 30 ◦ C/s with a residence time of 15 s under N2 flow. The products were analyzed by a GC-MS (Agilent GC7890A, 5975C MSD, USA) equipped with a DB-5MS capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 mm) under helium gas flow with 1 mL/min. The GC oven was heated from 40 ◦ C (held for 3 min) to 240 ◦ C (held for 15 min) at a heating rate of 10 ◦ C/min. The MS was operated in EI mode with an ionsource  electron  energy  of  70  eV.  The  detected  mass-to-charge  ratio ranged from 29 to 450. All of the chemicals were identified according to the NIST MS library [50].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  thermal  decomposition  behavior  of  the  BAHPPF-6FDA  type HPEI was characterized by TGA-MS. As shown in Fig. 3a, the first weight drop for HPEI begins at around 350 ◦ C. The weight loss between 350 ◦ C and 500 ◦ C  could  be  attributed  to  thermal  conversion  from  HPEI  to PEBO  accompanying  with  the  evolution  of  CO2 [45],  which  was confirmed  by  the  TGA-MS  curves  (Fig.  3b).  This  thermal-induced structure  change  can  also  be  proved  from  the  FT-IR  spectra  of TR-membranes (Fig. 4). The characteristic peaks of the imide ring (C -N stretching at 1388 cm 1 , C --O stretching at 1778 cm 1  and 1726 cm 1 ) of HPEI disappeared or obviously decreased. Simultaneously, the characteristic  peaks  of  oxazoles  appeared  at  1050  cm 1  and 1556 cm 1 , indicating that benzoxazoles ring was formed.

texts.orig: confirmed  that  the  phenol,  fluorene  and  9-phenyl-9H-Fluorene  were detected by GC/MS in the pyrolysis products of BAHPPF-6FDA (pyrolysis  at  550 ◦ C,  see  in  Fig.  4  and  Table  1).  These  products  undergo deoxygenation reaction during pyrolysis, resulting in the release of CO2 and H2O. The second CO2 release peak at 600-700 ◦ C may be related to the decomposition of the benzoxazoles units. And HCN was also detected during this stage. It was speculated that during the pyrolysis, the benzoxazoles units were first deoxidized to release CO2 and produce N -containing  segment  products,  which  are  then  decomposed  to  release HCN. The pyrolysis products including the benzonitrile and 2-pyridylbenzimidazole have been detected when the temperature up to 700 ◦ C (see in Fig. 4 and Table 1), indicating that the formation of the pyridine units ( N -containing fragment). In addition, more secondary aromatization products including biphenyl, dibenzofuran and phenanthrene were detected, indicating the formation of amorphous carbon structure in the membrane.  The  FT-IR  spectra  (Fig.  5)  of  the  CMS-550  membrane showed  the  characteristic  peaks  of  polymer  backbone,  including  the benzene ring (C --C, at 1598 cm 1 , 1495 cm 1  and 1473 cm 1 ), 6F unit (C -F,  at  1260-1210  cm 1 ),  ether  bond  (C -O -C,  at  1097  cm 1 )  and oxazoles  units,  indicating  that  its  microstructure  was  in  the ' mixedmatrix ' state between polymer and amorphous carbon. For the CMS-600 and CMS-700 membrane, however, these peaks were disappeared. This result  also  indicated  that  the  membrane  structure  has  transformed gradually from polymer chains to amorphous carbon. When the pyrolysis temperature was above 700 ◦ C, the weight loss gradually decreases, H2  was mainly released. Higher pyrolysis temperature leads to more complete  aromatization  and  forms  the  carbon  structure  with  locally ordered stacking.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. The effect of thermal rearrangement on CMS membrane performance
texts.text: 3.2. The effect of thermal rearrangement on CMS membrane performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As  mentioned  above,  the  structural  properties  of  polymeric  precursors have significant effects on the CMS membrane performance. For the HPEI precursor, during thermal treatment, it was converted into an intermediate polymer before the generalized decomposition of polymer backbone due to the occurrence of thermal rearrangement and the formation of rigid benzoxazoles segments. And the structural properties of the formed intermediate TR-polymers could be controlled by varying the thermal treatment protocols (e.g., temperature and soak time) [52,53]. It is speculated that thermal treatment protocols for preparation of intermediate TR-polymers maybe affect the derived CMS membrane performance. In this work, the structure of intermediate TR-polymer was tuned by prolonging the thermal soak time of HPEI at 450 ◦ C, and its influences  on  the  microstructure  and  gas  permeation  properties  of derived CMS membranes were investigated.

texts.orig: Fig.  6  shows  the  WAXD patterns of the HPEI and its TR-polymer membranes. All samples showed broad diffraction peaks, representing typical amorphous polymer structure. The calculated average interchain d-spacing value based on the peak position was listed in Table 3. The dspacing of the TR-polymers is larger than that of the HPEI due to the formation  of  benzoxazoles  units  in  membrane  [45].  During  thermal rearrangement, the random benzoxazole configurations of meta- and para-linkage lead to the formation of highly disordered chain structure and creation of more microvoids in the precursor membrane, resulting in the larger interchain spacing and smaller density value (Table 3). In addition, the d-spacing value of the three TR-membranes increases with the increase of the degree of thermal conversion, which was attributed to the increase of the rigid segment structure in the membrane.

The dspacing of the TR-polymers is larger than that of the HPEI due to the formation  of  benzoxazoles  units  in  membrane  [45].  During  thermal rearrangement, the random benzoxazole configurations of meta- and para-linkage lead to the formation of highly disordered chain structure and creation of more microvoids in the precursor membrane, resulting in the larger interchain spacing and smaller density value (Table 3). In addition, the d-spacing value of the three TR-membranes increases with the increase of the degree of thermal conversion, which was attributed to the increase of the rigid segment structure in the membrane.

texts.orig: were constructed based on the chemical structure of polymer monomer, and then generated single chain and amorphous polymer models [56]. The detailed simulation steps and more structure models can be found in SI. The predicted densities of amorphous polymer models match closely with experimental data, as listed in Table 3. This result implies that the simulated polymer chains were well equilibrated, similar to the actual polymer precursor membranes. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, the molecular conformation that near the imide ring was changed significantly due to the formation of a benzoxazoles structure. The molecular conformation behavior was represented by the analysis of the torsional angles. Fig. 8a and b shows the C -N Cph CphOH of torsional angle between the imide ring  and  the  ortho-hydroxyl  in  HPEI  chains  and  Cph Cph C -O  of torsional  angle  between  the  phenyl  and  benzoxazoles  in  TR-polymer chains, respectively. The range of C -N Cph CphOH torsional angles in HPEI chains is broadly distributed at 30 -170 ◦ (Fig. 8c), revealing that molecular conformation deviating from coplanarity. The Cph Cph C -O torsional angles have a smaller range of distribution in TR-polymer, indicating the greatly reduction the mobility and conformational state of polymer chain. And the lowest energy conformation in the  benzoxazoles  unit  is  closer  to  0 ◦ (Fig.  8b),  indicative  of  rigid coplanar molecular conformations. The thermal rearrangement to form rigid  benzoxazoles units, greatly reduces the mobility and conformational states of the polymer chain, which leads to narrower distribution of torsional angle. Moreover, the TR-polymer possesses bigger FFV than HPEI (Table 3, 17.4% for HPEI, and 21.6% for TR-polymer) because the rigid segment creates more microvoids in precursor membrane. The free volume morphology and size distribution of membranes were illustrated in  Fig.  8d.  After  thermal rearrangement, the FVD of precursor membranes become narrower and the distribution peak shifts to a larger size. These  results  are  consistent  with  WAXD  results,  indicating  that  the formation of rigid segments reduces the packing efficiency of polymer chains and increases the FFV of membrane.

TR450  (1h)-based  CMS-550  membrane  showed  a  higher  d002  value  of 4.71 Å than that of TR-450 (0h)-based CMS-550 membrane (4.57 Å), indicating that the carbon structure of this membrane is looser and more disordered. As mentioned above, the microstructure of CMS-550 membrane was in the ' mixed-matrix ' state, the rigid aromatic and heteroaromatic structures with great thermal stability were partially retained in the membrane, which favor the formation of contorted carbon layer structure  that  do  not  efficiently  pack  together.  For  the  TR-450  (1h) membrane, there are more rigid and thermally stable benzoxazoles units in the backbone, it causes the derived CMS membrane to have a looser and more disordered carbon structure and thus larger interlayer spacing. Moreover, the slightly lower d002 value (4.65 Å) of CMS-550 membrane derived from the TR-450 (2h) precursor compared to that of TR-450 (1h) precursor,  which may be attributed to the ' prepyrolysis ' of  polymer chain that thermal soaked at 450 ◦ C for 2h. Increased the pyrolysis time, although at low temperature of 450 ◦ C, would increase the densification degree of carbon structure in CMS membrane [24,57].

texts.orig: b, the CMS-550 membranes have the distribution of ultramicropores at 3 -4 Å, 4 -7 Å and micropores at 7 -15 Å. Compared to the TR-450 (0h)based  CMS-550  membrane,  TR-450  (1h)-based  CMS-550  membrane showed an enhanced pore volume (Table 4), and the pore size distribution shift towards higher pore size. These results in accordance with the WAXD results, suggest broadening of the pores. It is worth noting that the intensity of distribution peak at 7 -15 Å is enhanced significantly in TR-450 (1h)-based CMS-550 membrane, indicating the number of the micropores  ranging  at  7 -15  Å  increased. texts.orig: 3.3. The effect of pyrolysis temperature on CMS membrane performance
texts.text: 3.3. The effect of pyrolysis temperature on CMS membrane performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 11a shows the WAXD patterns of CMS membranes prepared at 550 ◦ C, 600 ◦ C and 700 ◦ C (derived from the TR-450 (1h) intermediate polymer). It can be seen that the (002) peak of CMS membranes moves towards the larger diffraction angles when the pyrolysis temperature increases, and the d 002 values decrease gradually. Fig. 11b illustrates the pore size distributions (3-15 Å) of these CMS membranes. Compared with CMS-550 membrane, the intensity of distribution peak at 7 -15 Å in CMS-600 membrane decreased, while that of ultramicropores ( < 7 Å) increased, especially at 4.5 -7 Å. As the pyrolysis temperature increase to 700 ◦ C, the intensity of distribution peak at 3 -4 Å increases considerably, while that of pores ranging at 4 -7 Å and 7 -15 Å decreases obviously. These results suggest that the shrinkage of pore structure of CMS membranes with the increase of pyrolysis temperature. The micropores gradually  shrink  to  ultramicropores  with  smaller  size,  and  the  pore volume in the CMS membranes decreases (Table 5). It will be beneficial to enhancing the permselectivity of CMS membranes in gas separation.

texts.orig: The pure gas permeation properties of CMS membranes pyrolyzed at 550 ◦ C, 600 ◦ C and 700 ◦ C were tested at 30 ◦ C and 0.11 MPa. As shown in Fig. 12, the gas permeabilities (except for the H2) of CMS membranes decrease with the increase of pyrolysis temperature, which in accordance with the decrease in the average interlayer spacing. The CMS-550 membrane showed the highest CO2  permeability of 17242 Barrer and moderated CO2/N2 (or CH4) selectivity, which was related to the loose carbon structure and large pore volume. The CO2  permselectivity increases with the increase of pyrolysis temperature (Table 5) due to the shrinkage of pore structure. For H2 molecule with little dynamic diameter of 2.89 Å, the highest permeability was obtained for the CMS-600 membrane,  which  may  be  attributed  to  the  suitable  ultramicropore size distribution. Although the total pore volume of 3 -15 Å in the CMS600  membrane  was  slightly  decreased  compared  to  the  CMS-550 membrane, the ultramicropore volume of 3 -7  Å  was  increased  obviously. This evolution for pore structure may be beneficial to enhancing H2  permeability of CMS membrane, which needs to be investigated in detail in the future work. For the CMS-700 membrane, the gas permeability decreased in the order of P (H2, 2.89 Å) > P (CO2, 3.3 Å) > P (N2, 3.64 Å) > P (CH4, 3.8 Å), which different the CMS-550 membrane and CMS-600 membrane, indicating that the molecular sieving mechanism dominates  the  gas  transport  through  membrane.  This  result  may  be attributed  to  sharply  shrinkage  of  membrane  pore  structure.  The obvious shift of pore distribution to smaller size was observed in CMS700 membrane (Fig. 11b), especially the significantly decrease of pore volume  that  pore  size  larger  than  6  Å,  which  caused  the  dramatic reduction  in  gas  permeabilities  of  CMS-700  membrane  compared  to CMS-600 membrane. And the average reduction rate of the gas permeability  increased  with the  increase  of  gas  molecular  size,  such  as  H2 (37%) < CO2  (78%) < N2  (87%) < CH4  (92%), which resulted in an obvious increase in gas selectivity.

texts.orig: 3.4. Mixed gas separation performance
texts.text: 3.4. Mixed gas separation performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixed  gas  permeability  and  selectivity  were  determined  using  a 50:50 mixture of CO2/N2  and CO2/CH4  for further assessing the practical application potential of CMS membranes. As shown in Fig. 13, the TR-450 (1h)-based CMS-550 membrane exhibited a mixed gas (CO2/N2 or CO2/CH4) selectivity over 20 along with a CO2 permeability of about 16500 Barrer. The relative decrease in CO2 permeability was attributed to the competitive permeation of the binary gases. Similar trends for CMS membranes were also observed in the previous work [43].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Aging of CMS membranes
texts.text: 3.5. Aging of CMS membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As we know, CMS membranes undergo aging, various molecules such as H2O and O2 in the environment would influence the pore structure of CMS membranes due to the physical or chemical sorption, resulting in the decrease of gas permeability [60]. Even in a vacuum or inert gas atmosphere, the shrinkage of pore structure in the CMS membranes was also observed over time [27,61]. The aging behavior significantly affects the performance stability of CMS membranes and reduces its service life. Therefore, the variation of gas separation performance with aging time (that  is,  performance  stability)  is  very  important  for  the  practical application of CMS membrane. Fig. 14 shows the variation of mixed gas separation performance of TR-450 (1h)-based CMS-550 membrane over time storage under vacuum. The membrane exhibited a decrease in CO2 permeability with an increase in CO2/N2  (CH4) separation factor over time. In detail, the CO2  permeability declined significantly during the first 30 days. After about 60 days, the CO2  permeability became about 60% of the fresh membrane and seemed to be reaching a stable value. The TR-450 (1h)-based CMS-550 membrane shows the relative high CO2 permeability  of  about  10000  Barrer  and  enhanced  CO2/N2  (or  CH4) selectivity  after  aging  60  days.  This  attractive  gas  separation  performance (Fig. 15) far exceeds the up bound of polymeric membranes [62], and is close to or better than that of aged CMS membrane reported in the reported literature [27].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considering the practical separation application, furthermore, the preparation  of  thin-skinned  membranes,  e.  g.  the  asymmetric  CMS hollow fiber [63] or CMS-porous substrate composite membranes [64] is needed. In the present work, the TR-450 (1h)-based CMS dense membrane with attractive performance especially for the high CO2  permeability showed great potential for CO2  separation application. For the high permeation CMS membrane, however, there are still some possible problems  that  need  to  be  avoided  when  converting  to  thin-skinned membrane form. Kamath et al. [63] reported the unusual aging phenomena in the asymmetric 6FDA/DETDA:DABA (3:2)-550 CMS hollow fiber membrane, that is, the drastic selectivity losses associated with the permeability reduction, which was not observed in its related dense CMS membrane.  It  was  believed  that  this  problematic  phenomenon  was attributed to the high residual stress in the fresh CMS, which originates from the cross-linked structure of precursor and could be eliminated by the esterification strategy in precursor structure design. For the HPEI precursor developed in present work, thermal-induced rearrangement reaction mainly occurred within the intrachain rather than between the interchains [45,56]. Although the degree of cross-linking in the HPEI precursor could be theoretically determined to be low, a similar aging tendency was observed in the CMS-550 membrane prepared in this work as in the dense 6FDA/DETDA: DAPA (3:2)-550 CMS membrane [27]. This  result  may  be  attributed  to  the  highly  disordered  carbon  layer structure (Fig. 9a) for the CMS-550 membrane prepared in present work. Based on the above considerations, we believe that the thin-skinned CMS  membranes  derived  from  HPEI  may  not  encounter  abnormal aging  phenomenon.  Of  course,  this  needs  to  be  confirmed  in  future research  works.  And  the  related  strategies  including  the  precursor structures design, pre and post-treatment to avoid the unusual aging in thin-skinned membranes would be studied in the later works.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, a novel HPEI (BAHPPF-6FDA) precursor was synthesized  and  employed  to  prepare  the  CMS  membranes  with  high  CO2 separation performance by varying the thermal treatment protocol and pyrolysis  temperature.  It  was  found  that  the  structural  properties  of intermediate TR-polymer, which depended on the thermal conversion degree of HPEI-to-PEBO, exhibited an obvious influence on the carbon structure, pore structure and CO2  separation performance of the CMS membranes. Increased thermal soak time of HPEI at 450 ◦ C leads to the increased  thermal  conversion  degree,  resulting  in  more  rigid  chain structure and increased FFV in polymeric precursor. The TR-450 (1h)based CMS-550 membrane exhibited a more open pore structure and higher  CO2 permeability  compared  to  TR-450  (0h)-based  CMS-550 membrane.  While  the ' prepyrolysis ' originating  from  the  too  long soak times at 450 ◦ C would cause the densification of the derived CMS membrane structure, resulting in the enhanced molecular sieving performance.  In  addition,  the  gradual  changes  in  the  CMS  membrane structure with pyrolysis temperature affected the resulting gas transport properties. The CO2 permeability of CMS membranes decreases with the increase of pyrolysis temperature due to the shrinkage of pore structure, while the CO2  permselectivity increases. The TR-450 (1h)-based CMS550  membrane  shows  the  highest  CO2  permeability  of  about  16500 Barrer  and  good  performance  stability  under  the  mixed  gas  (50:50 mixture of CO2/N2  or CO2/CH4) feed. The relative high CO2  permeability of about 10000 Barrer and enhanced CO2/N2 (or CH4) selectivity were obtained after aging of 60 days under vacuum, which exhibits a good potential for CO2 separation application.
